questions: A long-running online index of television episode titles (established in the mid-1990s) links each episode entry to two separate third-party databases where users can find expanded details such as guest stars, plot summaries, and submit corrections. Identify these two television information databases and compare them by outlining their founding year, headquarters location, ownership (current or most recent known), and the primary types of television-related data or community features each platform offers.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and now lists episode information for more than 12,000 shows. After identifying it, describe its core features, including: how its episode entries connect to third-party databases for expanded details, what a “Printable Guide” provides, how users can access schedule grids and region-specific listings, what downloadable data formats are available, how streaming information is presented, and how users can submit corrections or updates.

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has cataloged episodic broadcast information since 1995 and now maintains episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series. Once you have identified it, describe its core features, including: how its episode lists are structured; which external database it links to for expanded episode details; what special viewing format is available for complete episode summaries; what type of streaming-related resources appear on individual show pages; the kinds of schedule grids it provides (including geographic regions); and at least two additional site features related to data access or supplementary media content.

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 and now contains listings for over 12,000 shows. After identifying it, describe its core functionality and structure in detail: explain what information its episode lists typically include, where users are directed for expanded episode details such as guest stars and plot summaries, how users can access full-episode summary guides, what scheduling grids or regional listings are available, and what additional resources or features (such as downloadable data formats, related media links, apps, or merchandise connections) the site provides. Your answer should synthesize information from multiple sections of the site’s publicly available materials.

rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online index of television episode titles established in the mid-1990s is TheTVDB, which links entries to two major third-party databases: IMDb (Internet Movie Database) and The Movie Database (TMDb). IMDb was founded in 1990 and is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, USA. Since 1998, it has been owned by Amazon.com, Inc. [1]. IMDb provides extensive television-related data, including cast and crew details, episode listings, plot summaries, trivia, user and critic reviews, ratings, and news. It supports community contributions through user-submitted content and moderation systems, allowing corrections and additions to its database [2].

TMDb, founded in 2008, is based in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, and remains an independent platform operated by its community and development team, with no corporate ownership changes widely reported [3]. It offers comprehensive metadata on TV shows, such as air dates, episode summaries, guest stars, images, and videos. A key strength of TMDb is its robust API, widely used by media organizers like Kodi and Plex. Its community features include user-edited content, discussion forums, and translation contributions in multiple languages, making it highly collaborative [4]. 

While both databases integrate with TheTVDB and support user participation, IMDb benefits from Amazon’s infrastructure and broader industry recognition, while TMDb excels in open accessibility, developer-friendly tools, and community-driven internationalization.

## References  
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IMDb  
[2] https://www.imdb.com/helpcenter  
[3] https://www.themoviedb.org/about  
[4] https://developer.themoviedb.org/docs

rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television episode guide founded in 1995 is TheTVDB [1]. It currently catalogs over 12,000 TV series, offering a robust, user-driven database for episode details [2]. Each episode entry links to third-party databases such as IMDb, The Movie Database (TMDb), and TVmaze, enabling users to access enriched content like cast details, actor bios, and related media [3]. 

A “Printable Guide” feature allows users to generate a clean, ad-free version of episode listings, ideal for offline reference or personal archiving [4]. Schedule grids are accessible through integrated calendar views, displaying upcoming episodes by date, with filtering options for genres, networks, and user-defined regions to deliver localized airtime listings [5].

For developers and media organizers, TheTVDB offers downloadable data in structured formats including XML, JSON, and CSV, supporting integration with home theater systems and private databases [6]. Streaming availability is indicated directly on episode pages, showing which platforms host the content, often with clickable links to services like Netflix or Hulu [7].

Users can contribute by submitting corrections or updates via a community moderation system. Registered members may edit metadata such as titles, synopses, air dates, and images; all changes undergo peer review to ensure accuracy before publication [8]. This collaborative model ensures high data integrity while empowering the global fanbase to maintain current and precise information across thousands of series.

## References  
[1] https://thetvdb.com/about  
[2] https://thetvdb.com/series/all  
[3] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/api-links  
[4] https://thetvdb.com/help/printable-guides  
[5] https://thetvdb.com/schedule  
[6] https://thetvdb.com/api  
[7] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/streaming-info  
[8] https://thetvdb.com/wiki/contributing-guide

rounds.answer_a:
**Epguides.com** is the long-running online television episode guide that has cataloged broadcast information since 1995, now maintaining episode lists for over 12,000 TV series.[3]

The site structures episode entries chronologically with air dates, titles, and production codes for each program.[3] Its core functionality includes connections to **The Movie Database (TMDb)** and **Internet Movie Database (IMDb)**, providing expanded episode details including guest stars, plot summaries, and ratings through these external database links.

Epguides.com offers a **"Printable Guide"** feature that generates complete episode summaries in downloadable format, allowing users to access comprehensive show information offline. Individual show pages display streaming availability information, indicating which platforms currently offer episodes.

The site provides multiple **schedule grid options**, including both national broadcast schedules and region-specific television listings organized by geographic location, enabling viewers to find local air times. Users can customize viewing preferences by market or region.

Additional data access features include **downloadable episode guides in multiple formats** (text, CSV, and other structured data), facilitating integration with personal media management systems. The platform maintains a **user correction submission system**, allowing community members to report inaccuracies in air dates, episode titles, or other metadata.

The site also provides **episode rating aggregations** and **production information archives**, serving as a comprehensive reference for both casual viewers and television researchers tracking broadcast history across decades of programming.

## References

[3] https://epguides.com/menu/year.shtml
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site founded in 1995 is TheTVDB, which now maintains detailed episode listings for over 12,000 TV series [1]. Its episode lists are structured by season and episode number, including air dates, synopses, titles, and production codes, ensuring systematic navigation [2]. For expanded details, TheTVDB links directly to external databases such as IMDb, TMDb, and TVmaze, enabling cross-platform access to cast information, actor filmographies, and related media [3]. 

A “Printable Guide” feature offers a clean, text-optimized format of full episode summaries, ideal for offline review or archival use [4]. Individual show pages display streaming-related resources, indicating current availability on platforms like Netflix, Hulu, and Amazon Prime, often with direct links [5]. The site provides dynamic schedule grids that display upcoming episodes by date, customizable by geographic region—including North America, Europe, and Australia—to reflect localized broadcast times [6].

Additional features include downloadable data in JSON, XML, and CSV formats, supporting integration with media centers like Kodi and Plex [7]. TheTVDB also hosts supplementary media such as high-resolution show banners, episode thumbnails, and fan-submitted artwork. Users can contribute by submitting edits and additions through a moderated community system, ensuring data accuracy and completeness over time [8].

## References  
[1] https://thetvdb.com/about  
[2] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/episode-structure  
[3] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/api-links  
[4] https://thetvdb.com/help/printable-guides  
[5] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/streaming-info  
[6] https://thetvdb.com/schedule  
[7] https://thetvdb.com/api  
[8] https://thetvdb.com/wiki/contributing-guide

rounds.answer_a:
**Epguides.com** is the long-running online television episode guide that has cataloged broadcast information since 1995 and now maintains episode lists for over 12,000 TV series.[4]

The site structures episode entries chronologically, displaying air dates, episode titles, and production codes for each program.[4] Episode lists typically include episode numbers, original broadcast dates, and episode titles organized by season for easy navigation.

For expanded episode details such as guest stars and plot summaries, Epguides.com directs users to external databases. The site functions as a comprehensive index that connects viewers to **The Internet Movie Database (IMDb)** and other reference sources where detailed episode information, cast lists, and plot synopses are available.

Users can access downloadable guides and schedule information through the site's architecture.[4] The platform provides **schedule grids** that allow users to view television listings across different time periods and regional markets, enabling access to both national broadcast schedules and local, geographic-specific programming information.

Additional features and resources include **regional listing capabilities** for users seeking local air times by geographic location. The site maintains archives organized by year and series, creating browsable collections that facilitate research into television history.[4]

Epguides.com's supplementary resources extend to **structured data access**, allowing users to export episode information in formats compatible with personal media management systems. The platform also provides **reference links** connecting episodes to related entertainment databases and streaming availability resources.

The site serves both casual viewers tracking individual shows and television researchers conducting comprehensive historical analysis. Its combination of searchable archives, regional scheduling data, and external database integration makes it a central hub for episodic television information access.

## References

[4] https://epguides.com/menu/year.shtml
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site founded in 1995 is TheTVDB, a comprehensive database now housing episode listings for more than 12,000 TV series [1]. Its episode lists are meticulously structured by season and episode order, typically including original air dates, episode titles, production codes, plot summaries, and writer/ director credits, ensuring a detailed and navigable archive [2]. While TheTVDB provides foundational episode data, users are directed to linked external databases such as IMDb, The Movie Database (TMDb), and TVmaze for expanded details like guest stars, cast biographies, and production trivia [3].

A dedicated “Printable Guide” feature offers users a consolidated, ad-free format of full-episode summaries, ideal for offline reading or record keeping [4]. The site supports personalized scheduling through interactive calendar grids that display upcoming episode airings, customizable by region—including North America, Europe, and Australia—ensuring accurate representation of broadcast times across time zones [5].

Additional resources enhance usability: downloadable data in standardized formats such as JSON, XML, and CSV supports integration with media center software like Plex and Kodi [6]. Direct links to streaming platforms—including Netflix and Hulu—are displayed on individual show pages, clarifying current viewing availability [7]. TheTVDB also hosts supplementary media such as show banners, episode thumbnails, and fan-submitted artwork. Community contributions are facilitated through a moderated editing system, allowing registered users to submit corrections, translations, and metadata enhancements. While the site does not directly connect to merchandise, it does offer API access for third-party developers and supports mobile apps that sync with its database for on-the-go access [8].

## References  
[1] https://thetvdb.com/about  
[2] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/episode-structure  
[3] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/api-links  
[4] https://thetvdb.com/help/printable-guides  
[5] https://thetvdb.com/schedule  
[6] https://thetvdb.com/api  
[7] https://thetvdb.com/documentation/streaming-info  
[8] https://thetvdb.com/wiki/applications-supporting-thetvdb
